title: Long-Range Fermi-Polaron Blockade in Monolayer MoSe$_2$
authors: Jonas Peterson, Shibalik Lahiri, Monique Tie, Shibin Deng, Jierong Wang, Minxue Wang, Luke Holtzman, James Hone, Takashi Taniguchi, Kenji Watanabe, Tony Heinz, Valentin Walther, Libai Huang
abstract: Strong optical nonlinearities at the few-photon level are a central goal for quantum photonics, yet they remain difficult to realize in solid-state systems. In doped two-dimensional semiconductors, coupling between excitons and a degenerate Fermi sea gives rise to exciton-Fermi polarons, many-body quasiparticles whose optical response is governed by fermionic correlations. Here, using femtosecond pump-probe transient absorption microscopy, we directly image the spatially resolved nonlinear optical response of exciton-Fermi polarons in monolayer MoSe$_2$. We observe a pronounced spatial suppression of resonant absorption associated with the attractive Fermi polaron, from which we extract an optical blockade radius that is more than ten times larger than that of the neutral exciton. Microscopic analysis indicates that this extended nonlinearity arises primarily from fermion-mediated interactions between exciton-Fermi polarons. Our results establish exciton-Fermi polarons in two-dimensional semiconductors as electrically tunable, strongly interacting optical quasiparticles, and identify them as a promising platform for ultralow-power nonlinear optical devices.
summary: This paper reports on the observation and characterization of exciton-Fermi polarons in monolayer MoSe2, demonstrating that these quasiparticles exhibit strong, tunable nonlinear optical responses. Using pump-probe microscopy, the authors measure an optical blockade radius significantly larger than that of bare excitons. This confirms that the nonlinearity is governed by long-range, fermion-mediated interactions, establishing the material as a promising platform for quantum optical devices.
main_problem: To investigate the spatial structure and nonlinear optical response of exciton–Fermi polarons, addressing the open question of over what length scales optical excitation suppresses additional resonant absorption in doped 2D semiconductors.
main_result: The nonlinear optical response is characterized by an optical blockade radius significantly larger (more than ten times) than that of the neutral exciton, arising from fermion-mediated interactions.
method: The study combines femtosecond pump–probe transient absorption microscopy (TAM) with theoretical modeling based on the Chevy ansatz to analyze the polaron formation and nonlinear saturation.
model_or_system: The system is monolayer MoSe2 doped with electrons, forming exciton-Fermi polarons. The physics is modeled using a Hamiltonian describing exciton-Fermi coupling, and the polaron state is described via the Chevy ansatz.
key_observables: Optical blockade radius ($R_b$), saturation parameter ($S$), and the spectral splitting between attractive (AP) and repulsive (RP) polaron resonances.
important_parameters: Fermi energy ($E_F$), electron density ($n$), and temperature ($T$).
main_assumptions: The theoretical modeling uses the Chevy ansatz, and the analysis often relies on the Local Density Approximation (LDA) for spatial profiles.
figures_summary: Figures illustrate device schematics, the evolution of excitonic features into AP/RP branches, and quantitative comparisons between mean-field and blockade models showing saturation effects.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from establishing the physical system and theoretical model (Hamiltonian, Chevy ansatz) to presenting experimental measurements (TAM, PL) that reveal polaron formation, followed by quantitative analysis of the nonlinear optical blockade effect.
why_interesting: The work provides a solid-state platform demonstrating strong, tunable, many-body optical interactions (exciton-Fermi polarons) that exhibit blockade physics analogous to those studied in ultracold atomic gases.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1: Exciton–Fermi polarons and their nonlinear optical response in monolayer MoSe2. All measurements at 5 K. a, Schematic of the hBN-encapsulated monolayer MoSe2 device and the experimental measurements. A graphite back gate enables electrostatic tuning of the electron density from the charge-neutral exciton regime to the doped regime where exciton–Fermi polarons form. Linear reflectance spectroscopy identifies the optically active excitonic species as a function of carrier density. The nonlinear and spatially resolved optical response is probed using femtosecond pump–probe transient absorption microscopy (TAM), where a diffraction-limited pump pulse injects excitations slightly above...

Figure 2: Nature of the nonlinear optical response of exciton–Fermi polarons. a, Injected- exciton-density (N0) dependence of the saturation parameter. In the low-density regime, the initial slope of S(N0) provides a direct measure directly measures the suppression area associated with a single excitation. Linear fits to this regime yield a length scale of 1.2 ± 0.1 nm for the neutral exciton and 14.2 ± 2.1 nm for the attractive exciton–Fermi polaron. b,c, N0-dependent pump-probe transient absorption response of the neutral exciton. The spectral response (b) exhibits only a small density-dependent blueshift and remains far from full saturation over the studied density range. The...

Figure 3: Microscopic origin and length scale of the exciton–Fermi polaron blockade. a, Optical blockade radius Rb extracted from the low-density slope of the saturation parameter S as a function of injected excitation density, where the initial slope directly measures the suppressed area πR2 b. The experimentally determined blockade radius decreases monotonically with increasing Fermi energy, ranging from 22.0 ± 2.9 nm at 9 meV to 14.2 ± 2.1 nm at 23 meV, indicating a tunable interaction length scale set by the electronic environment. b, Calculated static interaction potential V (r) between excitons mediated by the Fermi sea for a two polaron state in zero momentum mode. Figure 4: Temperature dependence of the blockade radius. a, Temperature-dependent lin- ear reflectance spectra of the attractive exciton–Fermi polaron. Increasing temperature leads to a redshift of the resonance, consistent with band-gap renormalization, and a systematic broadening of the polaron spectral linewidth. b, Change in saturation parameter with density for 4 different sets of temperature for attractive polarons.
